title: The Domain Gap in NLP
concept: Pretrained models trained on general web text (Wikipedia) struggle on specialized domains (legal contracts, medical records) due to vocabulary shifts.

exercises.prompt: Before we start, do you know what NLP stands for in AI?
exercises.explain: NLP stands for Natural Language Processing, which is the field of AI focused on understanding human languages.

cards.title: Speaking Different Languages
cards.body: Have you ever tried reading a highly technical medical report or a complex legal contract? Even though it is written in English, it can feel like a completely different language. AI models experience the exact same struggle!

cards.title: What is the Domain Gap?
cards.body: A domain gap is the difference between the type of text an AI model was trained on (like Wikipedia) and the type of text it is asked to work on (like medical reports). When these two domains don't match, the AI gets confused.

cards.title: The Courtroom Translator
cards.body: Imagine a general English translator who is excellent at everyday conversations, but suddenly gets thrown into a courtroom. They will struggle to translate because legal contracts use words like 'heretofore' and 'indemnify'. The translator has a 'domain gap' between normal speech and legal speech.

cards.title: The Word 'Yield'
cards.body: In everyday web text, the word 'yield' might mean 'to give way' in traffic. But in a financial document, 'yield' refers to investment earnings. An AI trained only on general web text will likely misunderstand the financial meaning!

cards.title: Myth: AI Knows All English
cards.body: People think that since a model knows English, it can understand any English text. In reality, general AI models fail frequently on specialized fields like law, medicine, or coding because the vocabulary is completely different.

cards.title: Visualizing the Gap
cards.body: Look at how different the source training text is from the target text we want our AI to understand.

right.text: Clinical notes with abbreviations like 'cardiac arrhythmia'.

left.text: Wikipedia articles about cats, history, and science.

exercises.prompt: What kind of data are general pretrained language models typically trained on?
exercises.explain: General AI models are usually trained on vast, general databases of text like Wikipedia and news sites, which lack highly specialized terminology.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A model trained on Wikipedia will perform perfectly on medical reports without any extra training.
exercises.explain: If the target domain has very different words, the model's performance will drop. It needs special training to adapt.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the explanation of the domain gap.
exercises.sentence: AI models struggle on specialized fields because of shifts in the vocabulary.
exercises.explain: A domain gap occurs because the words and terms (vocabulary) used in specialized fields are very different from the everyday words found on general websites.

exercises.prompt: Identify which scenario represents a failure caused by a Domain Gap:

options.text: An AI specifically fine-tuned on thousands of pediatric reports.
A general web-chatbot trying to diagnose a patient using raw doctor notes.
A human doctor reading the medical records.

exercises.explain: An AI model designed for general web chat will not have the specialized training to accurately interpret complex medical records.

exercises.prompt: You are an AI engineer explaining a bug to your team. Your model, trained on Wikipedia, is failing to analyze hospital records. Which of the following describes this issue?

options.text: The time it takes to download a pretrained model over the internet.
The programming language difference between Python and JavaScript.
The performance drop when a general model runs on specialized text like legal contracts.
The physical distance between two server centers housing AI models.

exercises.explain: The domain gap happens when an AI model trained on general web text (like Wikipedia) encounters specialized data (like medical records) and struggles to understand the unique vocabulary and context.
